topics.title: Greedy Algorithms
topics.description: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to reach a global optimum. It works when the problem has the 'greedy choice property' (a local optimum leads to a global optimum) and 'optimal substructure' (optimal solution to a problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems). Classic greedy problems: activity selection, interval scheduling, jump game, coin change (certain denominations), Huffman coding.
topics.story: You're at a buffet with a limited plate size (constraint). Greedy strategy: always pick the highest-value food first. This works when foods don't conflict. But if you fill your plate with medium-value foods and miss the high-value ones that came out later — greedy fails. The key interview question: 'Can I prove greedy works here, or do I need DP?' For interval problems, sorting by end time makes the greedy choice provably optimal. For coin change with arbitrary denominations, greedy fails (need DP).
topics.analogy: Greedy = always take the best visible option now. Works: activity selection (take earliest-finishing meeting — proven optimal). Fails: coin change with [1,3,4] for target 6 — greedy picks 4+1+1=3 coins, but 3+3=2 coins is better. Proof technique: 'exchange argument' — show any other choice can be swapped for the greedy choice without worsening the solution. If you can prove this for each step, greedy is correct.
topics.memoryAnchor: Sort by end time for interval scheduling (maximize count). Sort by start time for merge intervals. Jump game: track maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]). Minimum platforms (trains): two sorted arrays (arrivals, departures), merge-style scan. Huffman: min-heap, merge two smallest repeatedly. Activity selection: sort by end, greedily pick non-overlapping. Coin change greedy only works for canonical denominations (powers, standard currency).
topics.firstPrinciples: Greedy correctness requires proof — usually by contradiction or exchange argument. Exchange argument: assume there's a better solution S* that differs from greedy G. Show that any difference can be swapped to match G without increasing cost. The exchange converges to G — so G is at least as good as S*. Without a proof, test with counterexamples before committing to greedy.
topics.bruteForce: Activity selection: try all subsets of non-overlapping activities — O(2ⁿ). Jump game: try all paths recursively — O(2ⁿ). Merge intervals: compare every pair — O(n²).
topics.optimizedApproach: Activity selection: sort by end time, greedily pick non-overlapping — O(n log n). Jump game: single pass tracking maxReach — O(n). Merge intervals: sort by start, merge overlapping in one pass — O(n log n). Minimum spanning tree: Kruskal's (sort edges) or Prim's (min-heap) — both O(E log V).
topics.whenToUse: Try greedy when: problem asks for max/min count of non-overlapping items, intervals, or scheduling. Red flags for DP instead: ask 'does the greedy choice ever need to be undone?' or find a counterexample. Greedy for: meeting rooms, jump game, task scheduling, Huffman, MST. DP for: coin change (arbitrary), 0/1 knapsack, edit distance, LCS.

examples.title: Interval Scheduling & Merge Intervals
examples.explanation: Sort by end time is the key insight: by always choosing the interval that ends earliest, we leave maximum room for future intervals. This is the exchange argument: if any other choice differs from greedy, swap it with the greedy choice — the result is no worse. Merge intervals sorts by start time (opposite!) because we want to combine overlapping intervals into one, not select non-overlapping ones.
examples.realWorldUse: Calendar: schedule max meetings in a room (activity selection). CI/CD: merge overlapping deployment windows. Database: merge overlapping date ranges for billing periods. Google Calendar's free/busy calculation merges overlapping calendar blocks.

examples.title: Jump Game I & II
examples.explanation: Jump Game I: track maxReach — if current index exceeds maxReach, we're stuck. No backtracking needed. Jump Game II: think in 'BFS levels' — currentEnd is the boundary of the current jump level. When we exhaust the current level (i == currentEnd), we must jump to the farthest reachable point. Meeting rooms: sort starts and ends separately — compare the next start vs the earliest end. If start < end, a new room opens. Otherwise, a room is freed.
examples.realWorldUse: Jump game = network hop count minimization. Meeting rooms = database connection pool sizing (max concurrent connections needed). Interval greedy patterns appear in cluster scheduling (Kubernetes pod scheduling), task dispatching (AWS Batch), and calendar optimization.

problems.title: Assign Cookies
problems.description:
Assign cookies to children: each child has a greed factor, each cookie a size. Assign one cookie per child, only if size >= greed. Maximize number of content children.

**Example 1:**
assignCookies([1,2,3], [1,1]) => 1
assignCookies([1,2], [1,2,3]) => 2

**Key insight:** Sort both. Use two pointers — give the smallest sufficient cookie to each child.
problems.pattern: Greedy, two pointers on sorted arrays
problems.hint1: Sort both arrays. Try to satisfy the least greedy child with the smallest sufficient cookie.
problems.hint2: Two pointers: child pointer and cookie pointer. If s[cookie] >= g[child], match them and advance both.
problems.hint3: If cookie too small, advance cookie pointer only (try next bigger cookie).

problems.title: Jump Game — Can You Reach the End?
problems.description:
Given jump lengths at each position, determine if you can reach the last index.

**Example 1:**
canJump([2,3,1,1,4]) => true
canJump([3,2,1,0,4]) => false

**Key insight:** Track `maxReach`. If current index > maxReach at any point, return false. Update maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]).
problems.hint1: Track maxReach = furthest index reachable so far.
problems.hint2: If current index i > maxReach, you're stuck — return false.
problems.hint3: maxReach = max(maxReach, i + nums[i]) at each step.

problems.title: Non-Overlapping Intervals — Minimum Removals
problems.description:
Remove the minimum number of intervals so the remaining intervals are non-overlapping.

**Example 1:**
eraseOverlapIntervals([[1,2],[2,3],[3,4],[1,3]]) => 1 (remove [1,3])
eraseOverlapIntervals([[1,2],[1,2],[1,2]]) => 2

**Key insight:** Sort by end time. Keep intervals with earliest end. If overlap, remove the one with later end (greedy: keep earliest end = most room for future).
problems.pattern: Greedy, sort by end time, activity selection
problems.hint1: Sort by end time. Greedily keep as many non-overlapping intervals as possible.
problems.hint2: Track lastEnd. If interval starts before lastEnd, it overlaps — skip it (count removal).
problems.hint3: Answer = total intervals - count of kept intervals.
